macro_segments.title: Opening Review: Houses and Stones Made for Idolatry and the Question of Worship

micro_segments.title: Summary of previous daf: stages of prohibition for a house or stone made for idolatry
The principle that an object becomes forbidden immediately upon being made for idolatry, even without worship
Cases where only what is added becomes forbidden (plaster, stones) while the original remains permitted
The concept of tashmish and what happens when an idol is placed in or on an object

macro_segments.title: The Mishnah on Three Types of Asherah Trees and Initial Classification

micro_segments.title: Three categories of Asherah trees: planted for idolatry, pruned with regrowth, and with an idol placed underneath
Case 1: Tree planted initially for idolatry becomes forbidden immediately
Case 2: Pruned tree—only what regrows is forbidden, not the original tree
Case 3: Idol placed under the tree—tree is permitted once idol is removed

macro_segments.title: Gemara Discussion: Rabbi Yannai's Qualification and the Meaning of Grafting

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yannai's view that actual grafting of a branch (not mere pruning) is necessary for the middle case
Reconciliation: Rabbi Yannai's statement applies to bittul, not to what becomes forbidden
The principle that grafting onto a tree does not redefine the entire tree

macro_segments.title: Shmuel's Position and the Debate Over Worship of a Growing Tree

micro_segments.title: Shmuel's view: bowing to a tree makes only what grows afterward forbidden
Rabbi Elazar challenges Shmuel using the Mishnah: pruning is necessary even after worship
The Gemara attributes Shmuel's position to Rabbi Yossi b'Rabbi Yehuda's opinion

macro_segments.title: Rav Ashi's Reframing: Reconsidering the Debate Between Rabbi Yossi and the Rabbis

micro_segments.title: Rav Ashi proposes that the debate concerns whether the original tree itself becomes forbidden, not just new growth
New proposal: all agree new growth is forbidden; the debate is whether the pre-existing base becomes forbidden
Rabbi Yossi b'Rabbi Yehuda says the entire tree becomes forbidden; the Rabbis say only the new growth
Proof from Scripture: ואשריהם תשרפון באש (burn them) vs. תגדעון (hew them down)

macro_segments.title: The Gemara's Resolution: Making Both Positions Work at Higher Levels

micro_segments.title: The Gemara proposes to accommodate both rabbi Yossi b'Rabbi Yehuda and the Rabbis at their revised positions
Rabbi Yossi b'Rabbi Yehuda: without pruning, the entire tree is forbidden; pruning reveals intent and limits prohibition
The Rabbis: just worshipping does not make new growth forbidden; pruning shows it does not make things worse

macro_segments.title: Defining Asherah: The Debate Between Rabbi Shimon and the Sages

micro_segments.title: Mishnah presents three cases; the first two are undisputed, the third is debated
Rabbi Shimon: only if the tree itself is worshipped is it forbidden; mere presence of an idol underneath does not make it forbidden
Story of Tzidon: finding an idol underneath a worshipped tree proves worship was directed to the idol, not the tree
The Sages: an idol placed underneath a tree makes it forbidden even without direct worship of the tree

macro_segments.title: Identifying a Tree as Asherah: Practical Signs and Shmuel's Extension

micro_segments.title: Rav's criterion: priests sit beneath it and do not eat its fruit
Shmuel extends the definition: even if priests use its fruit to make beer for their idol, it is forbidden
The principle that such signs point to worship (siman) rather than causing the prohibition

macro_segments.title: The Prohibition of Benefit from Asherah: Sitting in Shade and Ritual Impurity

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: do not sit in the shade of an Asherah; if you do, you remain pure
Discussion of direct shadow (shade of its stature) versus indirect shadow (shade of its shade)
Gemara clarifies: benefit is forbidden in all cases, but ritual impurity only applies if directly under the tree
Ritual impurity based on offerings to the tree: connection to the concept of death-impurity

macro_segments.title: Walking Under an Asherah: Public Domain, Alternative Routes, and Rabbinic Policy

micro_segments.title: If an Asherah overhangs public domain, it cannot forbid that space; the Rabbis have no right to forbid public land
Debate: may one walk under it if forced? Rav Isaac bar Eleazar says yes if no alternative; Rabbi Yochanan disagrees
Reconciliation: if an equivalent alternative path exists, one should not pass under; otherwise, one may
Story of Rav Sheshet: a prominent person should avoid lingering under an Asherah for propriety

macro_segments.title: Planting Under an Asherah: Benefit from Shade and from Fallen Leaves

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: vegetables may be planted under an Asherah during rainy season but not during sunny season
Lettuce (chazarim) cannot be planted under an Asherah in any season because shade always helps it
Rabbi Yossi: even in the rainy season, vegetables are forbidden because fallen leaves provide fertilizer (manure)
Gemara debate: does the combination of two contributing factors (soil and fallen leaves) render benefit forbidden?

macro_segments.title: Zeh v'Zeh Gorem: The Principle of Two Contributing Factors

micro_segments.title: The debate: Rabbi Yossi vs. the Sages regarding whether two sources of benefit can both be utilized
The apparent contradiction: Rabbi Yossi permits planting orlah that has been grafted but forbids benefit from an Asherah's fallen leaves
Explanation: in the idol destruction case, Rabbi Yossi is unconcerned because benefit is too indirect; here, the Rabbis view it macroscopically
Further clarification: the Rabbis permit planting vegetables because the harm from shade offsets the benefit from fallen leaves

macro_segments.title: Evidence of the Zeh v'Zeh Gorem Debate: Orlah and Animals Fed on Forbidden Food

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yossi permits planting an orlah sapling that has been grafted, implying zeh v'zeh gorem is permissible
Two contradictory baraitot about a field fertilized with forbidden manure and a cow fattened on forbidden food
The Gemara suggests these represent a dispute about zeh v'zeh gorem, but clarifies the positions are not between Rabbi Yossi and the Rabbis
Transition: the true debate about zeh v'zeh gorem lies elsewhere, to be resolved in the following discussion

topical_tags.term: Made for Idolatry (נטעו לשם עבודת כוכבים)
Rabbi Yossi b'Rabbi Yehuda (רבי יוסי ברבי יהודה)
